skill.name: firebase
skill.description: Guides Firebase architecture across Authentication, Firestore, Realtime Database, Cloud Functions, Storage, Hosting, security rules, Admin SDK use, and local emulators.
skill.summary: Plan and review Firebase apps with stronger data models, security rules, and cost controls.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Ruby shell backticks and weak cryptographic algorithms, but review found these are false positives in prose and Markdown formatting. No prompt injection, command execution instructions, malware intent, or data exfiltration behavior was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False positive: Markdown backticks are not command execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. SKILL.md line 56 uses backticks only to format related skill names. It does not instruct Ruby, a shell, or any tool to execute those names.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains only a related-skills list wrapped in Markdown code formatting. There is no executable Ruby context, shell syntax, or command invocation.

low_findings.title: False positive: weak cryptography terms are prose matches
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Static weak-crypto matches, including duplicate matches on line 3, occur in Firebase guidance text, not cryptographic code or implementation instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss Firebase backend scope, Firestore modeling, security rules, and query patterns. No cryptographic algorithm selection or weak hash usage is present.

content.value_statement: Firebase projects can fail through weak rules, poor data models, and unexpected costs. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan safer Firebase implementations.
actual_capabilities: Explains Firebase Authentication, Firestore, Realtime Database, Storage, Hosting, and Cloud Functions tradeoffs.
Guides Firestore data modeling around query patterns and denormalized reads.
Reviews security rule design concerns for Firebase data and storage access.
Identifies common cost risks such as broad listeners on large collections.
Suggests when to use the Admin SDK, emulators, and modular SDK imports.
limitations: Does not include runnable Firebase code files or deployment scripts.
Does not replace a full security review of production Firebase rules.
Does not inspect a live Firebase project or cloud configuration by itself.
Guidance is high level and may need current Firebase documentation checks.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Firebase App
use_cases.description: Design authentication, database structure, storage access, hosting, and functions before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Review Security Rules
use_cases.description: Check whether Firestore, Realtime Database, and Storage access patterns match the intended user permissions.

use_cases.title: Control Firebase Costs
use_cases.description: Find listener, query, and data modeling choices that can increase read volume or make migration harder.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Firebase Services
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose Firebase services for a new app with email login, user profiles, image uploads, and public pages.

prompt_templates.title: Design Firestore Collections
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Firestore data model for my product. Focus on query patterns, denormalized fields, indexes, and security rule boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Review Security Rules Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Firebase access model. Identify rule risks, client-side admin mistakes, and places where Cloud Functions should enforce privileged actions.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Architecture and Costs
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Firebase architecture for scale, cost, migration risk, emulator testing, Admin SDK usage, and production deployment readiness.

output_examples.input: A social app needs profiles, posts, likes, comments, and private messages.
output: Recommended collection boundaries based on reads and permissions.
Security rule concerns for private messages and profile updates.
Cost risks from broad post and comment listeners.

output_examples.input: A team wants to let users update subscription status from the client.
output: Warning that privileged billing changes should not run from client-side code.
Recommendation to use Cloud Functions or a trusted backend.
Rule review points for user-visible subscription fields.

output_examples.input: An existing app has slow Firestore screens and rising read costs.
output: Likely listener and query issues to investigate.
Data model changes that reduce repeated reads.
Emulator and monitoring checks before rollout.

best_practices: Start from security rules and query patterns before finalizing collections.
Use emulators to test authentication, functions, database rules, and storage rules together.
Keep privileged operations in trusted server code or Cloud Functions.
anti_patterns: Do not ship Firebase data stores with broad public read or write access.
Do not model Firestore like a relational database without checking query limits.
Do not attach real-time listeners to large collections without strict filters.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy Firebase projects?
faq.answer: No. It provides planning and review guidance, but it does not include deployment automation.

faq.question: Can it help with Firebase security rules?
faq.answer: Yes. It highlights rule design concerns and common access mistakes for Firestore, Realtime Database, and Storage.

faq.question: Does it cover Cloud Functions?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Cloud Functions as the place for trusted server logic and privileged operations.

faq.question: Can it reduce Firebase costs?
faq.answer: It can identify common cost drivers, especially broad listeners, repeated reads, and data models that create inefficient queries.

faq.question: Is it useful with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide Claude Code and Codex while they review or implement Firebase architecture in a repository.

faq.question: Does it replace current Firebase documentation?
faq.answer: No. Use current Firebase documentation for exact API behavior, limits, pricing, and deployment details.
